text: Body fat and training speed are the most important anthropometric and training predictors of race performance in endurance athletes when assessed via multivariate regression analysis.
Previous personal best time in shorter races is the single most important predictor of ultra-endurance race performance, outweighing anthropometric and training variables.
Lower body fat is associated with faster race performance across endurance disciplines including swimming, cycling, running, and triathlon.
Skin-fold thicknesses, body fat percentage, limb circumferences and lengths, body mass, body height, and BMI are all bivariately related to endurance race performance.
Ultra-endurance athletes train with higher volume and more total kilometers but at lower speeds compared to endurance athletes preparing for shorter events.
Higher training speed is associated with better race performance in endurance athletes and is one of the key predictors retained in multivariate models.
